original_hypothesis.title: Zero Lock Duration Enables Instant Lock/Unlock Cycle for Reward Gaming
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: When lockdrop.minLockDuration is 0 (no lockdrop configured or stale lockdrop with minLockDuration=0) and a user has not set their own lockDuration, the effective _lockDuration defaults to 0. This sets unlockTime = block.timestamp, allowing immediate unlock within the same block. Since forceHarvest is called in both lock and unlock, the user can accumulate schnibble rewards without meaningful time commitment.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The protocol's reward mechanism (schnibbles via forceHarvest) depends on the assumption that locked tokens remain committed for a duration. Zero-duration locks break this assumption and may allow reward extraction without real commitment.
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Lockdrop configured via start-lockdrop.ts with minLockDuration parameter (not visible in script); if lockdrop expires or was never configured, lockdrop struct defaults to all zeros including minLockDuration=0

proof_of_signal.pattern: _lockDuration = playerSettings[_lockRecipient].lockDuration; if (_lockDuration == 0) { _lockDuration = lockdrop.minLockDuration; } — both can be 0, leading to unlockTime = block.timestamp

preconditions: User deploys contract to call lock() then unlock() in same transaction (sequential, not reentrant)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: unlockTime = block.timestamp + 0 = block.timestamp. Unlock check: unlockTime > block.timestamp is FALSE, so unlock allowed same block.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Deploy helper contract that calls lock(token, amount) then unlock(token, amount) sequentially. forceHarvest called in both lock and unlock with locked weighted value briefly non-zero.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Depends on forceHarvest implementation — if rewards calculated per-second, 0 seconds = 0 rewards. If any per-transaction or per-call reward component, may be extractable.

economic_questions: Does forceHarvest grant any rewards for zero-duration locks?
Can flash loans amplify this by locking large amounts briefly?
Does getLockedWeightedValue contribute to reward calculations even within a single block?
validation_requirements: Analyze AccountManager.forceHarvest implementation for time-independent reward components
Test lock+unlock in same block via helper contract
Verify lockdrop struct defaults when no lockdrop configured

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Depends on flash loan availability — potentially large amounts for brief period
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Unknown — depends entirely on forceHarvest reward mechanics

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Flash loan liquidity for the token being locked

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All legitimate stakers (reward dilution if applicable)
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to attacker's flash loan size vs total locked value

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Lockdrop struct defaults; forceHarvest reward calculation model
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Determine if zero-duration lock yields any non-zero reward from forceHarvest

validation_result.mechanism: zero lock duration allows instant lock/unlock
validation_result.impact_summary: When no lockdrop is active and user has never set lockDuration, the effective lock duration is 0, allowing tokens to be locked and immediately unlocked in the same block. This bypasses the intended time-commitment requirement for the locking mechanism.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The _lock function in LockManager.sol derives _lockDuration from playerSettings[_lockRecipient].lockDuration, falling back to lockdrop.minLockDuration if the player setting is 0. When no lockdrop is configured (or the lockdrop has expired), the lockdrop struct defaults to all zeros including minLockDuration=0. For any user who has never called setLockDuration, both values are 0, resulting in _lockDuration=0. This sets unlockTime = block.timestamp + 0 = block.timestamp. The unlock function checks `lockedToken.unlockTime > uint32(block.timestamp)`, which evaluates to false when unlockTime == block.timestamp, allowing immediate unlock. The lockdrop-specific duration validation is inside a conditional block that only executes during an active lockdrop, so outside that period there is zero minimum duration enforcement. There is no other guard preventing a zero-duration lock. A helper contract can call lock() and then unlock() sequentially in the same transaction since nonReentrant resets between separate function calls.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is accurate. Lines 319-325 of LockManager.sol show the _lockDuration fallback logic where both playerSettings.lockDuration and lockdrop.minLockDuration can be 0. Lines 353-355 show unlockTime = block.timestamp + 0 = block.timestamp. Line 368 shows the unlock check that passes when unlockTime == block.timestamp. The lockdrop active check on lines 326-328 gates all duration validation, meaning outside lockdrop periods there is no minimum duration enforcement.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: When playerSettings.lockDuration is 0 (user never called setLockDuration) and lockdrop.minLockDuration is 0 (no lockdrop configured or lockdrop expired), _lockDuration remains 0. No further validation ensures _lockDuration > 0 outside of the lockdrop-active code path.

The duration validation is entirely gated by the lockdrop active check. When no lockdrop is active (start/end are 0 or lockdrop expired), this entire block is skipped, meaning _lockDuration=0 passes without any validation.
With _lockDuration=0, unlockTime is set to block.timestamp, which allows the unlock check (unlockTime > block.timestamp) to pass immediately.
When unlockTime == block.timestamp, the condition unlockTime > block.timestamp is false, so the revert is not triggered and the unlock proceeds immediately.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker deploys helper contract that will execute lock() then unlock() sequentially
2. Helper calls lock(tokenContract, quantity) - enters _lock() private function
4. _lock falls back to lockdrop.minLockDuration = 0 (no lockdrop configured)
5. lockdrop active check fails (start=0, end=0, not active), skip duration validation
6. ERC20 transferFrom moves tokens to LockManager
10. unlock checks: unlockTime(=block.timestamp) > block.timestamp => false, check passes
11. forceHarvest called (may or may not yield rewards)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: _lock at line 319 reads playerSettings[_lockRecipient].lockDuration which is 0. Line 321-323 checks if 0, falls back to lockdrop.minLockDuration which is also 0. Lines 326-328 check if lockdrop is active - it's not (default struct has start=end=0), so the entire duration validation block is skipped. Line 353-355 sets unlockTime = block.timestamp + 0. In unlock at line 369, unlockTime > block.timestamp is false (equal, not greater), so unlock proceeds.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: start-lockdrop.ts shows lockdrop is configured via startLockdrop action (not provided in scope) with start/end times. The minLockDuration parameter passed to configureLockdrop is not visible in the deployment script. However, the vulnerability applies in two certain scenarios: (1) before any lockdrop is configured (lockdrop struct defaults to all zeros), and (2) after the lockdrop expires (lockdrop.end < block.timestamp, making the active check fail). Both scenarios are inevitable during the contract's lifecycle. Additionally, configureLockdrop has no validation that minLockDuration > 0.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is straightforward: (1) Wait until no lockdrop is active (before first lockdrop or after it expires). (2) Deploy a helper contract. (3) Helper approves LockManager for token spending. (4) Helper calls lock(token, amount) then unlock(token, amount) in the same transaction. Both calls succeed because _lockDuration=0 sets unlockTime=block.timestamp, and the unlock check uses strict greater-than. forceHarvest is called in both lock and unlock with the locked weighted value briefly non-zero during the lock call. Whether this yields meaningful rewards depends on AccountManager.forceHarvest implementation (out of scope), but the zero-duration lock itself is a definitive code defect.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The code defect allows zero-duration locks, violating the fundamental assumption that locked tokens must be committed for a meaningful period. Impact depends on forceHarvest implementation: if rewards are time-based (per-second), impact may be minimal. If any per-call or per-lock component exists, rewards could be extracted without commitment. Additionally, getLockedWeightedValue returns non-zero during the brief locked period, which could affect external consumers. The defect also allows gaming via flash loans - lock large amounts, trigger forceHarvest with inflated getLockedWeightedValue, unlock immediately.
recommended_mitigations: Add a minimum lock duration check in _lock: require(_lockDuration > 0, 'Lock duration must be positive') or define a protocol-wide minimum lock duration in configStorage
Add validation in configureLockdrop that minLockDuration > 0
Consider using >= instead of > in unlock time check: require(block.timestamp >= lockedToken.unlockTime + 1) to ensure at least one block passes
Add a global minimum lock duration that applies regardless of lockdrop state
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LockManager with no lockdrop configured. 2. Configure an ERC20 token. 3. Register a test account via AccountManager. 4. Deploy attacker helper contract. 5. Approve LockManager for token spending from helper. 6. In a single transaction from helper: call lock(token, 1000), then call unlock(token, 1000). 7. Verify both calls succeed and tokens are returned. 8. Check if any schnibbles were awarded via forceHarvest.
uncertainties: The actual reward impact depends on AccountManager.forceHarvest implementation which is out of scope - if rewards are strictly time-proportional (per-second), zero-second locks may yield zero rewards
Whether flash loan sources exist for the configured tokens on Blast L2 to amplify the attack
The exact minLockDuration value used in production lockdrop configuration (though the vulnerability exists whenever no lockdrop is active)
additional_testing_needed: Analyze AccountManager.forceHarvest to determine if any rewards are granted for zero-duration or same-block locks
Test whether getLockedWeightedValue during the brief lock period affects any external reward calculations
Verify the default state of lockdrop struct when no configureLockdrop has been called
Test with flash-loaned amounts to measure maximum potential reward extraction
